serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of grilled saba spaghetti is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of cooked spaghetti topped with a grilled saba (mackerel) fillet and seasoning.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Mackerel, Atlantic, raw (FDC ID 175136) and Spaghetti, cooked, unenriched, without added salt (FDC ID 168901), combined in approximate ratio 35% mackerel to 65% spaghetti with minor oil and seasoning contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for grilled saba spaghetti exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Foundation Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values estimated by combining USDA FDC data for Atlantic mackerel (grilled, approximately 35% of dish weight) and cooked spaghetti (approximately 55% of dish weight) with a small contribution from olive oil and soy sauce seasoning (approximately 10%). Grilling assumed to reduce moisture by approximately 15% relative to raw mackerel values. Values represent a best-estimate composite per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; spaghetti (white, boiled) GI approximately 45–49; mackerel contributes negligible glycemic load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled saba spaghetti. GI estimated at 45 based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white spaghetti (GI approximately 45–49 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). Grilled mackerel and olive oil components contribute protein and fat which further attenuate postprandial glycemic response. GI of 45 is a conservative estimate; gi_category is 'low' (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 21.0 × 3.5) / 100 = 33.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving provides approximately 980 mg sodium, representing about 43% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or consume a smaller portion to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white spaghetti carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low GI of approximately 45 due to the protein and fat from grilled mackerel attenuating glycemic response, but the glycemic load per full serving is moderate at approximately 33 g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion or substitute wholegrain spaghetti to reduce postprandial blood glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acid content from saba mackerel
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saba (Atlantic mackerel) is rich in long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; this dish is a cardioprotective choice provided sodium from soy sauce is moderated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Saba Spaghetti is a Japanese-influenced fusion pasta dish featuring a grilled fillet of saba (Atlantic mackerel, Scomber scombrus) served over or tossed with cooked spaghetti, typically seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, olive oil, lemon juice, and spring onion. The dish is popular in Japanese-style Western restaurants (yoshoku) and contemporary Japanese cafés in Singapore and Japan. Saba is prized for its rich, oily flesh and pronounced umami flavour; grilling over high heat caramelises the skin and concentrates the fish's natural oils, producing a crispy exterior and moist, flaky interior. The spaghetti provides a neutral starchy base that absorbs the savoury pan juices and seasoning. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of high-quality protein (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g), omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA from mackerel), vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and niacin. The carbohydrate content is moderate (approximately 21 g per 100 g) and comes primarily from the spaghetti. Sodium is a concern due to soy sauce seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), moderated by the protein and fat content of the mackerel. Clinically, the omega-3 content supports cardiovascular health, while the moderate glycemic load requires portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for those with fish, gluten, or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: No direct nutrient entry for grilled saba spaghetti was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Foundation Foods, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FDC data for Atlantic mackerel and cooked spaghetti in approximate proportions representative of a restaurant-style dish. GI was estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (white spaghetti) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous data. Confidence is therefore low due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct source match.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for Atlantic mackerel (FDC ID 175136) and cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 168901) in an approximate 35:55 weight ratio with 10% contribution from olive oil and soy sauce. Grilling effect on mackerel moisture loss is accounted for by applying approximately 15% moisture reduction to raw mackerel values. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as Atlantic mackerel and plain spaghetti contain negligible industrial trans fats per USDA FDC data. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on white spaghetti GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); actual GI of the composite dish may be lower due to protein and fat from mackerel further attenuating glycemic response. Sodium estimate of 280 mg per 100 g reflects typical soy sauce seasoning in a restaurant preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA FDC component data and are not directly measured for this dish. The dish is served at GyoGyo - Funan, Singapore, at SGD 19.84. The menu name '456D Grilled Saba Spaghetti(D)' suggests a menu item code; canonical food name normalised to 'Grilled Saba Spaghetti'. Halal status is not confirmed for this restaurant; is_halal set to false pending verification.
